en.extract:
The Bougainville thrush (Zoothera atrigena) is a species of bird in the thrush family Turdidae. It is found in the montane forests of Bougainville Island in the Solomon Islands Archipelago.
It was formerly considered to be conspecific with the New Britain thrush with the combined taxa known as the black-backed thrush.


Taxonomy
The Bougainville thrush was formally described in 1982 by Dillon Ripley and Don Hadden from a specimen collected at an altitude of 1,500 m (4,900 ft) on Bougainville Island, Papua New Guinea. They considered it to be a subspecies of the black-backed thrush (now renamed the New Britain thrush) and coined the trinomial name Zoothera talaseae atrigena. The epithet atrigena combines Latin ater meaning "black" with gena meaning "cheek". The Bougainville thrush is now considered to be a separate species based on its distinctive morphology.

The '''Bougainville thrush''' ('''''Zoothera atrigena''''') is a species of bird in the thrush family [[Turdidae]]. It is found in the montane forests of [[Bougainville Island]] in the [[Solomon Islands (archipelago)|Solomon Islands Archipelago]].

It was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[New Britain thrush]] with the combined taxa known as the black-backed thrush.

==Taxonomy==
The Bougainville thrush was [[species description|formally described]] in 1982 by [[Sidney Dillon Ripley|Dillon Ripley]] and Don Hadden from a specimen collected at an altitude of {{cvt|1500|m}} on [[Bougainville Island]], [[Papua New Guinea]]. They considered it to be a [[subspecies]] of the black-backed thrush (now renamed the [[New Britain thrush]]) and coined the [[trinomial name]] ''Zoothera talaseae atrigena''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Ripley | first1=S.D. | author1-link=Sidney Dillon Ripley  | last2=Hadden | first2=D. | date=1982 | title=A new subspecies of ''Zoothera'' (Aves: Muscicapidae: Turdinae) from the northern Solomon Islands | journal=Journal of the Yamashina Institute for Ornithology | volume=14 | pages=103-107 | doi=10.3312/jyio1952.14.103 | doi-access=free }}</ref> The epithet ''atrigena'' combines [[Latin]] ''ater'' meaning "black" with ''gena'' meaning "cheek".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=atrigena | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=atrigena | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=23 March 2025 }}</ref> The Bougainville thrush is now considered to be a separate species based on its distinctive morphology.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Thrushes | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/thrushes/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=23 March 2025 }}</ref>
